items.title: Diagnose underfitting before regularizing
items.avoid: Applying heavy L2, dropout, or shallow trees when both train and validation error are already high.
items.prefer: Plot learning curves; if train error is high, increase capacity or features first, then tune regularization.
items.why: Regularization increases bias — useful only after the model can fit training data reasonably well.
items.example: Neural net with train loss plateau: try wider layers before raising weight decay from 1e-4 to 1e-1.

items.title: Match model family to known structure
items.avoid: Defaulting to linear models for clearly nonlinear or interaction-heavy domains without testing.
items.prefer: Use domain knowledge to pick features or architectures (trees for tabular interactions, CNNs for spatial data).
items.why: Correct inductive bias lowers bias without arbitrary parameter inflation.
items.example: Tabular churn with category crosses: gradient-boosted trees often beat plain logistic regression on the same raw columns.

items.title: Use validation error to pick complexity
items.avoid: Choosing the most complex model because ' bigger is always better.'
items.prefer: Grid-search depth/degree/width; select the simplest model whose validation error is near the minimum.
items.why: Past the bias floor, extra complexity mostly buys variance and compute.
items.example: Polynomial degree 3 minimizes val MSE; degree 12 lowers train error but raises val MSE — stop at 3.

items.title: Separate bias from irreducible noise
items.avoid: Assuming any remaining error must be fixable with a bigger model.
items.prefer: Estimate a Bayes-error floor (label noise, measurement error); stop when train error approaches that floor.
items.why: Chasing noise with capacity increases variance without reducing true bias.
items.example: Human annotators disagree 5% of the time — val accuracy above ~95% may be impossible regardless of model size.
